titleStmt.title: Letter from Alexander Crawford to Sir Matthew Nathan, 28 March 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Alexander G. Crawford

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Alexander G. Crawford to Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939). Crawford is continuing his correspondence with Nathan about the pension of a Derry school principal, Mr Houston. Crawford informs Nathan of the sacrifices Houston had made during his career and suggests that he deserves the pension grant offered by the school's main benefactor, the Irish Society. Mathew Nathan had become under-secretary in 1914 under Augustine Birrell (1850-1933). After the Easter Rising 1916, Nathan resigned as under-secretary due to his misjudgement and failure to stop the Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/5498/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Academical Institution Coleraine March 28. 1916 Sir, I am in receipt of
yours of 25th inst. (4904) Might I take the liberty of
pointing out that during a
large part of Mr. Houston's
Principalship, he was entitled
to a percentage of the school
fees but refrained from claim=
ing it && received instead the
balance on hand at the end
of the year, thus suffering con-
siderable loss. The Grants from the Irish
Society were made largely to
relieve Mr. Houston of
financial strain. Would it not meet the
case if the Irish Society ear-
marked Â£100 of these grants for
Mr. Houston, besides the Â£200 which
they have voted for his pension?
I know that the Agent of the Society
would be agreeable to the arrang-
ment, as it would carry out the
intentions of the Society. In case that it is necessary
to have an amended Scheme,
what would would the approximate
cost of the same be to the Governors? Believe me, Your obedt. servant, Alex G. Crawford
Hon Secty. The Under Secretary Dublin Castle.
